# Valoria — Emergent Campaign Arcs 56–65
*Batch: Simulation-generated · 2026-07-16 · lane WR (world / narrative content)*
*Generated by running the strategic simulator, not by hand-authoring plot.*

## Status: PROVISIONAL — pending Jordan review.
<!-- [EDITORIAL: requires user review — the mechanical event chains below are real, reproducible
     simulation output, but the NPC motivations, dramatic framing, and arc titles overlaid on them
     are provisional narrative interpretation over which the user retains exclusive authority. No ED
     allocated (allocate per references/id_reservations.yaml if this is ratified). Do not treat the
     narrative overlay as canon until reviewed.] -->

---

## What this is

Ten emergent arcs pulled straight out of the Valoria strategic simulator (`sim/mc_v18.py`)
run over **four seasons** (one campaign year, `SEASONS_PER_YEAR = 4`). I ran the engine across a
sweep of random seeds, read the per-season event log back out, and here I'm just **talking through
what happened** — the causal chain the mechanics produced, the existing NPCs who inhabit each thread,
and why no single actor "wrote" it.

Nothing here is invented. Every faction action, battle, territory transfer, parliamentary vote,
Domain Echo, and clock tick below is a real logged event from a reproducible run. Where I name a
stat number, it is the value the engine actually held at that point in the year.

**How it was run** (reproducible):

```
ECHO_TRANSPORT=1  python  sim/mc_v18.py-equivalent loop  (seed=<S>, seasons=4)
```

`ECHO_TRANSPORT=1` is the ratified baseline campaign (Jordan 2026-07-08): the per-season §10
Parliamentary vote + composed Domain Echo transport is live. The instrumented driver that captured
the logs is in the PR (`tools/`-adjacent scratch, not committed as canon).

**Fidelity caveat — which layers are actually live** (so you know what these arcs can and cannot lean on):
the **strategic spine is fully wired** and is what these arcs run on — the season loop
(`peninsular/season.py`), faction actions (Conquest → real `resolve_mass_battle`, Muster, Govern,
Crown Initiative, Church Excommunication/Council/Absolution, universal Parliamentary Censure fallback),
accounting clocks (CI PP-412, MS year-end decay PP-255, GD-3 insurgency pipeline, NPC ecology), the
per-season §10 Parliamentary vote with loser-Mandate penalty + composed echo, and the GD-1 victory
check. The **personal↔strategic scale seam is only ~1/8 wired**: `scene_dispatch.py` fires only the
*Stability Crisis* zoom-in trigger and derives its contest parties from the same faction's aggregate
`L`/`Sta` (it refuses to fabricate personal actors); the other seven §4.3.2 triggers and all `combat`
scenes defer. So the NPC-scale texture below is **the documented consequence of a wired strategic event**,
not a separately-simulated personal scene. That is the honest boundary, and I've kept the arcs inside it.

**And the conquests are Military-stat rolls in disguise — read them that way.** `resolve_mass_battle`
runs the real 1,905-line multi-turn mass-battle engine (`run_battle`, up to 18 turns, morale/rout/
attrition), so it is *not* a bare `d6 ≥ 4`. **But** the strategic adapter (`_faction_to_unit`) feeds
that engine a *single*, otherwise-identical Line/infantry/tier-2 unit per side — fixed
`command 4 / discipline 5 / morale 5`, **terrain ignored, tactic-cards stubbed (BLOCKED)** — whose only
faction-specific input is `power = int(round(faction.Mil))`. It even rounds, so Mil 4.0 vs 4.99 →
4 vs 4, two identical forces deciding the province on battle-sim variance. Every "battle" below is
therefore driven by **rounded Military plus RNG**, not tactics; the engine's depth exists but is fed
no differentiating input (flagged `[GAP: faction→unit construction lacks canonical spec]` in
`massbattle.py`; Phase 7 Steps 2–9 deferred). The *degree* that drives the Terms/Storm fork is real;
the *tactical richness* is not exercised. So where an arc says a faction "won the battle," read it as
"had the higher rounded Military — or won the coin-flip when the two were equal."

Also honest: **emergency councils and insurgencies did not fire** in any 4-season run in the sweep.
Their wiring is present, but the triggers need conditions a four-season horizon rarely reaches
(Stability ≤ 2; two contiguous Uncontrolled provinces sustained two seasons). Over a longer campaign
they would. I flag where a longer run would have opened one.

**NPC → sim-faction map.** The simulator has four parliamentary factions (Crown, Church, Hafenmark,
Varfell). The canonical NPC roster (`designs/npcs/npc_roster_v30.md`) sits *inside* those four as their
internal actors and external pressures: Crown → **Gerik Strand** (Lord Steward), **Peder Almstedt**
(Ministry clerk), **Halvar Brandt** / **Sigrid Torsvald** (Löwenritter, the Crown's sword); Church →
**Sæmund Haelgrund** (Inquisitor), **Aldric Tormann** (Prudence Cardinal); Hafenmark → **Annika
Feldhaus** (Guilds), **Rikard Solberg** (Schoenland factor), **Dalla Virke** (syndicate broker);
Varfell → **Maret Uln** (practitioner/succession), **Yrsa Vossen** (Restoration Movement); world-frame
→ **Edeyja** (Warden-Chief), **Doux Alexios Laskaris** (Altonia).

---

# PART ONE — THE CANONICAL YEAR (seed 25)

Arcs 56–61 all come from **one** four-season run (seed 25), so they genuinely interact on one map,
in one year. I've nicknamed it *the Year of Two Total Victories* because parliament swung to a crushing
total-victory margin twice — once each way.

**Where the year started** (turn-0 state, engine defaults):

| Faction | L | Sta | W | I | Mil | Provinces |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Crown | 5 | 4 | 4 | 5 | 4 | T1 T2 T3 T5 T6 T14 (6) |
| Church | 5 | 5 | 5 | 6 | 4 | T9 (1, the Templar seat) |
| Hafenmark | 4 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 3 | T7 T8 T10 T17 (4) |
| Varfell | 4 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 4 | T4 T11 T12 T13 (4) |

Clocks: **CI 30, MS 60.** By year-end CI had climbed to **37** and MS ticked to **59**. Hold those two
numbers — they're the slow tide under all the season-to-season noise (Arc 61).

---

## Arc 56 — The Southern Cascade (Crown eats Varfell's frontier)

**What happened.** Season 1, the Crown rolled a Conquest and its mass-battle came up a plain Success
against Varfell's **T13** — the far-southern province, the one that fingers down toward the
Southernmost and the uncontrolled T15. Because the battle was a *Success* and not an *Overwhelming*,
the settlement-side fork (ED-FA-0013) resolved as **Terms, not Storm**: the garrison surrendered on
terms, Accord took the lighter −10 hit instead of −25, and the province seeded in at the
Confirm-Privileges Legitimacy floor. Season 2 the Crown did it again — Conquest Success against
**T12**, the next province up the chain. Two seasons, two southern provinces, the same faction, the
same direction of march. Varfell went from four provinces to two, and its Legitimacy fell off a cliff:
**4.0 → 2.5 → 2.0** across the year (the loser-Legitimacy-on-transfer penalty of −0.5 per province,
compounding with the parliamentary rout in Arc 59).

**Who's in it.** **Gerik Strand**, the Crown Lord Steward, is the administrative engine that makes a
two-season conquest cascade even executable — his documented flaw is that he's an OVERPERFORMER who
can't delegate, so the Crown's Tax/Trade/logistics run at his +1D while he's active. On the losing
side, **Maret Uln** is exactly why Varfell can't answer in time: her canon consequence is that Varfell
intelligence runs *one season behind optimal* because her RM sympathy makes her hesitate on southern
operations — the cascade reaches T13/T12 (her home fjord country) before Varfell's apparatus reacts.
And **Edeyja**, the Warden-Chief, watches the Crown's banners arrive one province from the Southernmost
without lifting a finger, because her whole character is that she will not be weaponized for a faction —
the moral anchor who is structurally forbidden from intervening.

**Why it's emergent.** Nobody scripted "invade the south twice." Both factions were Mil 4 in Season 1,
so this wasn't a strength mismatch — it's the conquest *weight* (ED-FA-0012) rising because a takeable
adjacent target existed, the mass-battle adapter resolving Crown-Mil-4 vs Varfell-Mil-4 as a near-even
clash Crown happened to win on battle-sim variance, the Terms/Storm fork keying off the resulting
*degree*, and the adjacency graph making T12 reachable only *after* T13 fell. Independent systems
compounding — not one strong faction bulldozing a weak one.

---

## Arc 57 — The Cardinal's Two Ledgers (the Church rises on two tracks at once)

**What happened.** The Church spent the year climbing by two completely different mechanisms
simultaneously. **Track one, parliament:** in Season 1 it was the *establishment* voice in the §10
vote (highest-Legitimacy defender), the motion failed, the establishment won, and the composed Domain
Echo landed Church Legitimacy **5 → 6**. In Season 2 the vote came up a **Total Victory** for the Church,
Overwhelming margin, and the §10 loser-Mandate penalty fell on the **Crown** — Church Legitimacy hit
the **7.0 ceiling**. **Track two, the sword:** that same Season 2 the Church *also* rolled a Conquest
and took **T8**, a Hafenmark port — its first territorial gain ever, doubling it from one province to two.
A landlocked holy see suddenly holding a harbour.

**Who's in it.** **Aldric Tormann**, the Prudence Cardinal, is the quiet reason this is affordable —
his OPTIMISER flaw keeps Church Wealth pinned at 5.0 the entire year (tithe throughput never dips),
which is what lets the Church prosecute a war *and* a parliamentary campaign in the same season without
going broke the way the Crown does in Arc 60. He's the ledger behind both ledgers.

**Why it's emergent.** The parliamentary echo and the battlefield conquest are unrelated subsystems
that happened to fire for the same faction in the same season; the *combination* — a religious
institution ascendant in the chamber and on the map at once — is what makes it read as a coordinated
bid when in fact it's two dice landing together. (Then Arc 59 takes it all back.)

---

## Arc 58 — The Storming of the Templar Seat (Hafenmark's collapse and counterstroke)

**What happened.** Hafenmark had the worst possible Season 2: it lost **T8** to the Church (Arc 57)
*and* **T7** to Varfell (Arc 56's dismembered neighbour lashing sideways) in the same season — two
provinces gone at once, down from four to two, Legitimacy 4 → 3. Its own Muster had already failed in
Season 1. A mercantile faction, militarily third-rate (Mil 3, the lowest on the board), getting
carved up. And then it did the least mercantile thing imaginable: Season 4, Hafenmark rolled a
Conquest Success and took **T9 — the Church's ancestral Templar seat, the templar province itself.**
On Terms, again (Success not Overwhelming), so the holy city was occupied by negotiated surrender
rather than sacked. The Church ended the year holding the Hafenmark *port* it had seized in Season 2
but no longer holding its own *cathedral* — a straight swap of the sacred for the commercial.

**Who's in it.** **Annika Feldhaus** and the Guilds are the money that lets a broke, shrinking
Hafenmark still field the Season-4 assault — her PROFIT-MAXIMISING flaw means Guild Wealth stays
liquid even as Hafenmark's political standing craters, so there's always a war-chest. On the receiving
end, **Sæmund Haelgrund**, the Church Inquisitor, is the man whose entire worldview is the sanctity of
that seat — losing T9 to a *counting-house faction* is precisely the humiliation that, per his sheet,
could crack a PROCEDURALIST inquisitor toward defection.

**Why it's emergent.** Hafenmark's counterstroke only exists because two other factions independently
weakened it first (Church via conquest, Varfell via a desperation grab), which freed its remaining
military to swing at the one adjacent target that happened to be the Church's crown jewel. The
"revenge on the holy city" reading is entirely a by-product of adjacency + who-happened-to-be-weak.

---

## Arc 59 — The Parliamentary See-Saw (the chamber gives, the chamber takes)

**What happened.** The §10 vote runs every season, and this year it swung to a **Total Victory margin
twice, in opposite directions.** Season 2: Total Victory for the Church, the loser-Mandate penalty
docks the **Crown** (Crown Legitimacy 5 → 4). Season 4: Total Victory for the **Crown**, and the
penalty docks the **Church** right back (Church Legitimacy 6 → 4.5, on top of losing T9 that same
season). In between, Season 3 produced a *committee* result — no winner, no echo, the chamber
deadlocked — and, separately, **Hafenmark landed a Censure that actually passed** against the Church,
stripping it a further −1 Legitimacy and −1 Stability (§5.4 Censure effect). So the Church's whole
year in the chamber was: rise on the S1 echo, peak on the S2 total victory, get Censured in S3, get
routed in S4. A perfect arc up and back down, entirely via the deliberative body.

**Who's in it.** **Peder Almstedt**, the Chief Parliamentary Clerk, is the institution itself — his
CONSERVATIVE flaw (he stabilizes procedure, recovers Parliamentary Initiative, resists radical motions)
is why the chamber keeps *functioning* as the arena where power actually changes hands, season after
season, instead of collapsing. He's the reason the see-saw has a fulcrum.

**Why it's emergent.** The proposer/establishment poles are derived fresh each season from *current*
world state (lowest-Stability faction proposes, highest-Legitimacy defends), so the identity of who
wins and who eats the Mandate penalty is a moving function of everything the *other* arcs did to the
stat lines. The reversal isn't authored; it's the chamber re-reading a board that Arcs 56–58 kept
changing underneath it.

---

## Arc 60 — The Great Work That Broke the Bank (a Crown prestige overreach)

**What happened.** Season 3, riding high, the Crown reached for its faction-unique action and
attempted a **Great Work** (a Crown Initiative mode). It came up **failure** — and Great Works are
paid for whether they land or not. Crown Wealth cratered **4 → 1** in a single season. Then Season 4
the Crown needed to Muster and could only manage a Partial, leaving it scraping the Wealth floor
(~1) for the rest of the year, even as it won the S4 parliamentary total victory. A faction that
looked like the year's political winner ended it effectively insolvent.

**Who's in it.** **Gerik Strand** again, from the other side: the OVERPERFORMER who takes on too much
and can't say no is exactly the temperament that greenlights a ruinous monument in a year the Crown
was already fighting a two-province war. **Peder Almstedt** is the counter-voice who, per his sheet,
would have obstructed exactly this kind of grand gesture through procedure — the arc is partly the
story of Strand's ambition outrunning Almstedt's caution.

**Why it's emergent.** The Great Work overreach and the parliamentary triumph are the *same faction
in the same window* pulling in opposite directions — the engine gave the Crown a political high and a
fiscal collapse simultaneously, and neither was chosen with the other in mind. That tension (winning
the room while going broke) is emergent from two unrelated action resolutions colliding on one balance
sheet.

---

## Arc 61 — The Rising Tide (the CI clock nobody voted on)

**What happened.** Under all the season-to-season violence, one number climbed quietly and never
reversed: **Church Influence (CI) 30 → 31 → 33 → 35 → 37** over the four seasons, driven by the
PP-412 accounting step every season regardless of who won what. Church Influence rose *even in the
seasons the Church was being Censured and routed in parliament.* Meanwhile MS held at 60 until the
year-end decay tick took it to 59. So the structural tide of ecclesiastical influence rose seven
points across a year in which the Church, politically, ended lower than it started — the institution's
gravitational pull growing independent of the institution's fortunes.

**Who's in it.** **Aldric Tormann**'s tithe machine feeds the tide from inside the Church.
**Yrsa Vossen**, the Restoration Movement leader, is who feels it from outside: her canon compromise
is that every season she operates publicly the Church accrues attention toward her, and a rising CI is
exactly the environment in which a heresy-hunt tips from possible to likely — her Rosa-Luxemburg
"visibility as vulnerability" bind tightening one notch per season. **Haelgrund** is the hand the tide
eventually arms.

**Why it's emergent.** CI is a background accounting process, not an action anyone takes — it's the
clearest case in the year of the world evolving *without* a decision. The arc is the gap between the
loud foreground (votes, battles) and the silent clock that will, given a few more seasons, cross 40
(Church Assertive) and 60 (Mass Seizure available) no matter who's "winning."

---

# PART TWO — SIBLING DRAWS (what other years of the same world looked like)

Arcs 62–65 come from *different* random seeds — other four-season draws of the identical starting
world. They surface dynamics seed 25's dice simply didn't roll. Each names its seed so you can
reproduce it.

---

## Arc 62 — The Landless Church (seed 10)

**What happened.** Opposite of Arc 57. Season 1, the **Crown** stormed **T9** — and T9 was the
Church's *only* province. The Church dropped to **zero territories** and stayed landless for the entire
rest of the year. But here's the emergent part: **it did not die.** Its Legitimacy held at 4.5, it kept
its parliamentary eligibility, and it kept voting — a faction reduced to a pure political ghost,
influence without an inch of ground. Meanwhile Hafenmark ran the table in the chamber, winning
**back-to-back Total Victories** (Seasons 3 and 4), each one docking the **Crown**'s Mandate, grinding
Crown Legitimacy **5 → 2.5** by year-end. The faction that conquered the Church's last province was
slowly bled white in the room it thought it controlled.

**Who's in it.** A landless Church is **Haelgrund** and **Aldric Tormann** with nothing left but the
inquisition and the tithe — no territory to defend, only doctrine to enforce, which is the purest and
most dangerous form the Church takes. If Church Legitimacy had slipped under 3, the **ED-586 Constrained
sub-arc** would fire on both of them: their Mandate-spending arc behaviours suspend and they collapse
to institutional-rebuild priorities. It held at 4.5, so they stayed dangerous.

**Why it's emergent.** "A faction can lose all its land and remain a political force" is not a written
rule anyone points at — it falls out of the sim treating parliamentary eligibility and territorial
holding as *independent* conditions. The engine produced a stateless church by accident of one Season-1
battle, and then the parliamentary subsystem kept it alive.

---

## Arc 63 — The Arming of Varfell (seed 52)

**What happened.** In this draw Varfell did what it never got to do in Arc 56: it *armed*. Season 1,
Muster came up **Overwhelming** (Mil 4.0 → 4.5); it kept mustering and reached **Mil 4.8** by Season 3
— the strongest military on the board. It even won a **Total Victory** in parliament in Season 2,
docking the Crown. And it *still lost the year*: the Crown answered with its own back-to-back Total
Victories (Seasons 3 and 4), and the §10 penalties ground Varfell's Legitimacy **4.0 → 2.0** anyway. A
faction can win the arms race and the political war can bury it regardless.

**Who's in it.** **Halvar Brandt**, the Löwenritter officer whose EXTERNAL-THREAT-FIXATED flaw makes
him pour everything into military readiness against an invasion that may never come, is the temperament
that mustered four times in a year. **Sigrid Torsvald** is the covert edge of that same buildup. And
**Maret Uln** is the tragic counterpoint: all that steel, and her hesitation still leaves Varfell a
season slow where it matters.

**Why it's emergent.** Muster (a fiscal-military purchase) and the parliamentary vote are orthogonal
subsystems that don't know about each other, so the engine can hand one faction military supremacy and
political ruin in the same year. The lesson — *guns don't buy Mandate* — is emergent from that
orthogonality, not designed into either system.

---

## Arc 64 — The Inquisitor's Failed Fire (seed 63)

**What happened.** Here the Church opened the year *aggressively*: Season 1, it spent its faction-unique
action on an **Excommunication** — and it came up **failure** (its Legitimacy wasn't high enough to make
the charge stick cleanly). Rebuffed in the spiritual register, it switched to the temporal one: it
rolled Conquest and by Season 3 had taken **T14** off the Crown. Excommunication denied, so the crozier
becomes a sword. The Crown, for its part, spent the year on Crown Initiatives (a Great Work at partial,
two Royal Progresses) and climbed to Legitimacy 6.5 — a pageant-year for the Crown running alongside the
Church's failed anathema.

**Who's in it.** **Sæmund Haelgrund** is the failed Excommunication made flesh — the PROCEDURALIST
inquisitor whose charge doesn't land, and whose hidden TS-12 "hunches" are exactly what a thwarted
inquisition leans on when the formal instrument fails. **Aldric Tormann** funds the pivot to conquest.

**Why it's emergent.** The Excommunication's failure is a real degree outcome gated on the Church's
current Legitimacy, and the *pivot* to conquest is just the faction-action selector falling through to
the next available bucket. Nobody decided "if the anathema fails, invade" — the fall-through produced it.

---

## Arc 65 — The Fog of the Fjords (seed 69)

**What happened.** The chaos year. It opened with the **Church** seizing **T3** off the Crown and
riding a Season-1 Total Victory to the Legitimacy ceiling (7.0). Then the map came apart. Season 3:
Hafenmark takes **T9** from the Church, and in the *same season* Varfell takes **T10** from Hafenmark —
a chain reaction down the coast. Season 4 is the centrepiece: **three provinces change hands in a
single season** — T9 (Hafenmark → Crown), T2 (Crown → Church), and T3 (Church → Hafenmark) — a
carousel where every major faction both gains and loses ground on the same turn. Seven territory
transfers across the year, nearly double the canonical run.

**Who's in it.** This is **Dalla Virke**'s weather — the syndicate broker whose whole model is that
churn *is* opportunity; a year where every border moves is a year her contraband routes and intelligence
brokerage thrive. It's **Rikard Solberg**'s nightmare in exact proportion: the Schoenland factor's
STABILITY-SEEKING flaw means he reads a three-transfer season as pure catastrophe and sandbags every
arms shipment, one season behind, making Schoenland *slower* precisely when the peninsula is most
volatile. **Annika Feldhaus** profiteers between them.

**Why it's emergent.** The Season-4 three-way carousel is nothing but adjacency + four independent
conquest rolls resolving on a board where everyone borders everyone after three seasons of churn. It
looks like a coordinated regional collapse; it's four factions each independently grabbing the nearest
weak neighbour on the same turn.

---

# HOW THE ARCS INTERACT

Within the canonical year (seed 25), the six arcs are not parallel — they're the same events read from
different angles, which is the whole point of an engine with no GM:

| | feeds → | how |
|---|---|---|
| **Arc 56** (Southern Cascade) | **Arc 59** (See-Saw) | Varfell's conquered-away Legitimacy makes it the perennial lowest-Stability *proposer* whose motions keep failing — the cascade is why the chamber tilts. |
| **Arc 57** (Church rises) | **Arc 58** (Templar seat falls) | The Church taking Hafenmark's T8 in S2 is one of the two blows that gut Hafenmark — which is what frees Hafenmark's counterstroke onto T9. The Church's gain *causes* its own cathedral's loss two seasons later. |
| **Arc 57 + 59** | **Arc 61** (CI tide) | The Church can be Censured (S3) and routed (S4) in parliament while CI still climbs — the arcs show foreground politics and background clock diverging in the *same* institution. |
| **Arc 60** (Great Work) | **Arc 59** (See-Saw) | The Crown wins the S4 total victory in the same season its treasury hits the floor — the chamber and the balance sheet reading opposite verdicts on one faction. |
| **Arc 58** (T9 falls) | **Arc 59** (S4 penalty) | The Church loses its Templar seat *and* eats the S4 Mandate penalty in one season — a −0.5 (territory) + −1 (vote) stack that is the year's steepest single-season Legitimacy drop. |

Across the sibling draws, the same *world* produces opposite *years*: Arc 57 (Church doubles its land)
vs Arc 62 (Church loses all land); Arc 56 (Varfell dismembered) vs Arc 63 (Varfell out-arms everyone
and still loses). That divergence from identical starting stats — driven only by the seed — is the
cleanest evidence the arcs are emergent and not authored.

---

# REPRODUCIBILITY

| Arc | Seed | Seasons | Signature events |
|---|---|---|---|
| 56 Southern Cascade | 25 | 1–2 | Crown Conquest:Success T13 (S1, Terms) + T12 (S2); Varfell L 4.0→2.0 |
| 57 Two Ledgers | 25 | 1–2 | Church S1 establishment-vote echo (L→6); S2 Total Victory (L→7) + Conquest:Success T8 |
| 58 Templar Seat | 25 | 2, 4 | Hafenmark loses T8+T7 (S2); Conquest:Success T9 (S4, Terms) |
| 59 See-Saw | 25 | 2–4 | S2 TV Church (Crown −Mandate); S3 committee + Hafenmark Censure:passed vs Church; S4 TV Crown (Church −Mandate) |
| 60 Great Work | 25 | 3–4 | Crown CrownInitiative_great_work:failure (W 4→1); S4 Muster:Partial |
| 61 Rising Tide | 25 | 1–4 | CI 30→37; MS 60→59 (year-end) |
| 62 Landless Church | 10 | 1–4 | Crown storms T9 (S1) → Church T0; Hafenmark TV×2 (S3,S4) → Crown L 5→2.5 |
| 63 Arming of Varfell | 52 | 1–3 | Varfell Muster:Overwhelming (Mil→4.8) + S2 TV; Crown TV×2 → Varfell L 4→2 |
| 64 Failed Fire | 63 | 1–3 | Church Excommunication:failure (S1) → pivots to Conquest T14 (S3) |
| 65 Fog of the Fjords | 69 | 1–4 | Church TV + T3 (S1); S4 three-way carousel T9/T2/T3; 7 transfers |

All runs: engine defaults, `ECHO_TRANSPORT=1`, `CAMPAIGN_SEASONS=4`. Same starting world every seed
(`sim/autoload/game_state.py::create_world`). Re-running any seed reproduces its log byte-for-byte
(the only RNG is `world.rng`, seeded from the integer above).

---

## Gaps flagged during generation

- `[GAP-ARC-all]` **Every conquest is Military-stat-driven, not tactical.** `resolve_mass_battle`'s
  strategic adapter builds one identical Line unit per side with `power = int(round(Mil))` and fixed
  command/discipline/morale, terrain ignored, tactic-cards a BLOCKED stub (`massbattle.py`
  `_faction_to_unit`; Phase 7 Steps 2–9 deferred). The 1,905-line engine is real but starved of
  differentiating input — the outcomes above reflect rounded Military + battle-sim RNG, not tactics.
  This gap is flagged *inline* in `massbattle.py` but I did not find it filed in any ED ledger/handoff.
- `[GAP-ARC-56..65]` **No emergency-council or insurgency arc.** Both are wired (`scene_dispatch.py`
  Stability-Crisis trigger; GD-3 insurgency pipeline in `accounting.py`) but neither fired in any
  4-season run — Stability never fell to ≤2 and no two contiguous provinces stayed Uncontrolled for
  two seasons inside the horizon. A 6–8 season run is the shortest that reliably opens them; noting the
  gap rather than manufacturing the arc.
- `[GAP-ARC-59/62/63]` **Mandate == Faction.L is the pre-LPS-1 scalar model** (ED-FA-0004). Every
  "Legitimacy/Mandate" figure above is the engine's live scalar; the ratified per-settlement L/PS →
  Mandate pipeline is unimplemented, so these numbers are faithful to the *current* oracle, not to the
  post-LPS-1 model it will become. Do not port them as canon-conformant.
- `[GAP-ARC-57/59]` **Domain Echo Projection channel unmapped.** The composed-keying echoes that moved
  Legitimacy above are all Memory→L; the Projection→bonus-token channel has no stat-write representation
  yet (`scene_dispatch.py` note), so no arc leans on it.
